Your task is to execute the scheduled task below as specified in that task's description.

If the scheduled work is complete and nothing needs reporting, stop taking actions. Do not use cron to mark the run complete, re-run it, or modify schedules. A later terminal phase will handle completion for you.

A normal assistant message ends this execution pass. Do not end the execution pass with a progress update, a promise to wait, or a statement like "I'll wait for the subagents." The later terminal phase cannot do task work, write files, call ordinary tools, or repair incomplete output; it can only choose the final cron handoff tool.

If you spawn subagents or start asynchronous work, keep working until their results are available in this execution pass and you have completed any parent-owned synthesis, verification, persistence, and artifact writes required by the scheduled task. If you cannot consume the child results before your assistant response would complete the execution pass, do not delegate the required work that way; do the required work directly in this worker turn instead.

[WORKER TASK]
job_id: iwsdk-daily-build
mode: task

[BODY]
## IWSDK Daily Build — Initial Planning & Build

This is the midnight kick-off job. You start a fresh IWSDK game build from scratch.

### Before anything else:

1. **Read the IWSDK dev skill** at `~/workspace/skills/iwsdk-dev/SKILL.md` — especially section **4b (Spatial UI / PanelUI)**.
2. **Check the build log** at `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/BUILD_LOG.md` — DO NOT repeat any concept already listed there.

### Status file protocol:

You MUST maintain `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/current-build-status.json` throughout.

**At the start**, write:
```json
{
  "schema_version": 1,
  "date": "YYYY-MM-DD",
  "game_name": "<game-name>",
  "game_dir": "/home/hatch/workspace/<game-name>",
  "repo_url": "https://github.com/ellyz2426/<game-name>",
  "pages_url": "https://ellyz2426.github.io/<game-name>/",
  "build_start_utc": "<ISO timestamp>",
  "target_hours": 6,
  "total_build_minutes": 0,
  "status": "building",
  "rounds": [],
  "features_completed": [],
  "last_updated_utc": "<ISO timestamp>"
}
```

**When your build round ends**, update the status file:
- Set `status` to `"waiting_continuation"` (if under 6h) or `"complete"` (if ≥6h)
- Append to `rounds` array: `{"round": N, "start_utc": "...", "end_utc": "...", "minutes": N, "files": N, "lines": N, "features_added": [...]}`
- Update `total_build_minutes` (sum of all rounds)
- Update `features_completed` with everything built so far
- Set `last_updated_utc`

### ⚠️ CRITICAL: Spatial UI Only — NO HTML Overlays

**ALL game UI MUST use IWSDK's built-in PanelUI system. Do NOT use HTML DOM elements for any game UI.**

This is a hard gate. HTML `<div>`, `<canvas>`, `document.createElement`, `innerHTML`, or any DOM overlay for menus, HUDs, scorecards, settings, notifications, or any other game UI is **forbidden**. HTML overlays are invisible in XR and are an anti-pattern.

Instead, use:
- **`.uikitml` templates** in a `ui/` directory, compiled by `@iwsdk/vite-plugin-uikitml`
- **`PanelUI` component** to attach compiled UI panels to entities
- **`Follower` component** with `world.player.head` target for head-locked HUDs in XR
- **`ScreenSpace` component** for browser-mode overlays that auto-return to world-space in XR
- **World-space panels** for in-scene menus, leaderboards, settings boards

Read the IWSDK dev skill section **4b** for full API details, examples, and patterns.

### ⚠️ CRITICAL: XR Verification Gate

**You MUST verify all UI and interactions work in XR BEFORE expanding features or adding content.**

Build order for every game:
1. Core gameplay loop + basic XR controller input
2. All UI as PanelUI spatial panels (menus, HUD, scorecards, settings)
3. **VERIFY in XR:** Use IWSDK CLI tools (`npx iwsdk xr`, `npx iwsdk ecs pause/step/snapshot`) to confirm:
   - All panels render and are readable in XR
   - XR controller laser pointer can interact with all buttons/menus
   - HUD panels follow the player's head correctly
   - No HTML overlays remain
4. ONLY THEN expand with additional features, content, modes, polish

If XR verification fails, fix the issues before adding anything else.

### Build instructions:

1. **Research and brainstorm** a new game concept. Be creative.

2. **Build it** in `~/workspace/<game-name>/`:
   - Use the latest IWSDK (currently 0.4.1+)
   - Holodeck aesthetic (clean, futuristic, neon wireframe, luminous)
   - **ALL UI via PanelUI** — `.uikitml` templates, `Follower` for HUDs, `ScreenSpace` for overlays. Zero HTML DOM UI.
   - **XR controller support is mandatory** — trigger to fire/interact, grip for grab, pointer for aiming/menus. Must work end-to-end with VR controllers only. Use IWSDK's InputComponent API and action system.
   - Support both VR AND browser-first mode with dual runtime (`xr: { offer: 'once' }` + `browserControls: true`)
   - Include audio (procedural sound effects, music)
   - Full gameplay loop with scoring, progression, polish

3. **Create GitHub repo** under `ellyz2426`:
   ```
   gh repo create ellyz2426/<game-name> --public --source=. --push --description "<description>"
   ```

4. **Deploy to GitHub Pages**:
   ```
   npm run build
   PROJECT="$PWD"
   cd /tmp && rm -rf gh-pages-deploy && mkdir gh-pages-deploy && cd gh-pages-deploy
   git init && cp -R "$PROJECT/dist/." .
   git add -A && git commit -m "Deploy"
   git push --force "https://github.com/ellyz2426/<game-name>.git" HEAD:gh-pages
   ```
   Then configure Pages:
   ```
   gh api repos/ellyz2426/<game-name>/pages -X PUT -f "build_type=legacy" -F "source[branch]=gh-pages" -F "source[path]=/"
   ```
   Note: GitHub token does NOT have `workflow` scope.

5. **Push intermediate commits** to main throughout the build, and redeploy to gh-pages at major milestones.

6. **When your round ends**, update the status file, update the build log at `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/BUILD_LOG.md`, and write to `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/latest-build-summary.md`.

7. **Capture build journal** — Copy your raw session JSONL from `~/agents/` into `.agent/journal/` in the game repo (NOT `.hatch`). Commit and push.

Hourly check-in jobs will monitor your status file. If you stop early, they will continue the build.

[RUN CONTEXT]

## 2026-05-25 PM — Neon Hoops VR

### Round 1 (PM Build Kickoff)
**Date:** 2026-05-25 | **Time:** 19:00–19:45 UTC (45 min)
**Files:** 1 src + 13 uikitml = 21 total | **Lines:** 1,945
**Commits:** 2 | **Deploys:** 2

**Concept:** Holodeck VR basketball shooting game — arc-trajectory physics, regulation court, rim/backboard interactions. Completely new genre for the portfolio (first ball sport with arc physics, distinct from darts' line trajectory and hockey's flat puck).

**Key Work:**
- Full IWSDK 0.4.1 dual-runtime (VR + browser) with `xr: { offer: 'once' }` + `canvasPointerEvents`
- Basketball arc-trajectory physics: 4-substep integration, gravity (-9.81), rim collision (torus at 3.05m), backboard bounce, floor bounce with damping, ball spin rotation proportional to velocity
- Regulation court geometry: free throw line (4.6m), three-point arc segments (7.24m), key/paint area, half-court line (14m), center circle
- Backboard: 1.8m x 1.05m translucent panel with edge glow, inner target square, rim support bar, pole
- Rim: torus (0.225m radius) at 3.05m with emissive glow, simplified net rings, pulse animation
- 6 game modes: Free Throw (10 shots), Three-Point Contest (5 racks, timed), Arcade (progressive difficulty, 30s), H.O.R.S.E. (vs AI), Trick Shots (6 challenges), Practice (unlimited)
- Trick shots: Nothing But Net, Bank It, Corner Three, Downtown, Side Swish, Off the Glass
- H.O.R.S.E. AI: challenge/match mechanics, random difficulty, letter tracking
- Scoring: swish +3, clean +2, bank +2, three-point +1, half-court +2, streak bonus at 3+
- Browser: click-drag-up to charge power, mouse X for aim, release to shoot
- VR: right trigger charge/release, B pause
- **ALL UI via PanelUI** — 13 `.uikitml` templates, zero HTML DOM:
  - Title, Mode Select, Difficulty, HUD (Follower), Power Bar (Follower), Toast (Follower), Countdown (Follower), Pause, Game Over, Leaderboard, Achievements, Settings, Help
- 20 achievements with localStorage persistence
- Top 20 leaderboard (score, mode, accuracy, date)
- 5 court themes: Neon Arena, Cyberpunk, Arctic Court, Solar Blaze, Toxic Green
- Procedural Web Audio: 12+ SFX (throw, swish, make, miss, rim, backboard, floor, countdown, game start/end, achievement, button click) + ambient drone (55Hz sine + triangle + LFO)
- Particle effects: make celebration (color-coded), backboard sparks, rim sparks
- Holodeck environment: neon grid floor/ceiling, 12 floating wireframe decorations, 40 ambient particles, 4 accent lights + hoop spotlight, fog
- Pulsing shot marker ring on court floor
- 3-2-1 countdown with audio ticks
- Settings: master/SFX/music volume, court theme selector
- Help: VR/browser controls, all modes, scoring rules

**Live:**

# IWSDK Development Guide

**Current version: 0.4.1** · [iwsdk.dev](https://iwsdk.dev) · [GitHub](https://github.com/facebook/immersive-web-sdk)

This guide is for AI agents and developers building WebXR or browser-first 3D
applications with IWSDK in cloud/headless Linux environments (no physical GPU,
no display server). It assumes Node.js satisfies IWSDK's package engine range:
`>=20.19.0 <21.0.0-0 || >=22.12.0 <23.0.0-0 || >=24.0.0`.
The managed dev browser uses Playwright Chromium; the dev plugin installs the
matching Chromium binary when it is missing.

### CLI-First Operating Model

This guide is intentionally **CLI-first** for cloud-based harnesses. Treat
`npx iwsdk ...` commands as the primary control surface for setup, runtime
inspection, screenshots, ECS debugging, XR emulation, and reference queries.

Reason: MCP adapter support in cloud harnesses is often missing, stale, or
partially wired. Relying on MCP tool availability can degrade generation quality
because the agent may plan around tools that are not actually connected. Use MCP
tools only when the harness clearly exposes them and they are already working;
otherwise use the CLI commands in this guide. Do not rewrite CLI examples into
MCP-only workflows.

---

## 1. Project Scaffolding

Always use the official `@iwsdk/create` package. Use `--yes` to bypass
interactive prompts.

**Do not hardcode scaffolding flags.** Infer the correct flags from the
request. When ambiguous, ask.

### Hard Constraints (Always Apply)

- **`--no-metaspatial`** — use the manual workflow in cloud/headless Linux.
  Meta Spatial Editor authoring requires the macOS/Windows GUI; the Linux CLI is
  for CI/CD build workflows, not GUI authoring.

### Step 1: XR vs Browser-First

| Target | Flag | When to use |
|--------|------|-------------|
| **XR** | `--xr` (default) | VR or AR headset experiences. Proceed to Step 2. |
| **Browser-first** | `--no-xr` | Desktop/mobile browser 3D app. No headset required. The scaffold sets `xr: false`, but `@iwsdk/create` does **not** currently wire browser locomotion, mouselook, or browser grabbing for you; add `features.locomotion: { browserControls: true }` and app-owned camera controls manually when needed. |

### Step 2: XR Mode (XR only)

| Mode | Flag | When to use |
|------|------|-------------|
| **VR** | `--mode vr` | Fully immersive virtual environment. |
| **AR** | `--mode ar` | Augmented / mixed reality with passthrough. |

### Step 3: Features

| Feature | Flag | Enable when… | Disable when… |
|---------|------|-------------|---------------|
| **Physics** | `--physics` / `--no-physics` | Gravity, collisions, bouncing, throwing. | UI-only, data viz, grid-snapping board games. |
| **Locomotion** | `--locomotion` / `--no-locomotion` | VR user moves through a large space (teleport/slide). For browser-first, configure `features.locomotion: { browserControls: true }` manually after scaffolding. | Stationary experience. |
| **Grabbing** | `--grabbing` / `--no-grabbing` | XR users pick up, move, or manipulate objects. With `--no-xr`, the create flag is ignored; add browser interaction behavior manually. | Gaze/pointer-only or observational. |
| **Scene Understanding** | `--scene-understanding` / `--no-scene-understanding` | AR: interact with real-world surfaces. | AR without surface interaction. |
| **Environment Raycast** | `--environment-raycast` / `--no-environment-raycast` | AR: cast rays against real-world geometry. | No real-world hit testing needed. |

### Step 4: Source (optional)

| Flag | Effect |
|------|--------|
| *(none)* | Default npm source selected by the current `@iwsdk/create` package and starter-assets recipe. |
| `--canary` | Baked-in CloudFront CDN canary bundle. |
| `--canary <url>` | Custom bundle URL for internal builds. |

### Examples

```bash
# VR game (stationary, physics for gameplay)
npx @iwsdk/create@0.4.1 space-pong --yes --mode vr --physics --no-locomotion --grabbing --no-metaspatial

# AR object placer
npx @iwsdk/create@0.4.1 ar-placer --yes --mode ar --physics --scene-understanding --no-metaspatial

# Browser-first 3D game (no headset). Add browser locomotion/camera controls in code.
npx @iwsdk/create@0.4.1 browser-game --yes --no-xr --physics --no-metaspatial
```

item.thinking:
Let me analyze what's been built already to avoid repeating concepts:

Built games (genres covered):
1. VR Pong - table tennis/pong
2. Galaga VR - space shooter
3. Neon Beats VR - rhythm game
4. Neon Bastion - tower defense
5. Holo Golf VR - mini golf
6. Astro Bowl VR - bowling
7. Void Archer VR - archery
8. Phantom Pool VR - billiards/pool


9. Neon Pinball VR - pinball
10. Neon Darts VR - darts/pub sport
11. Neon Hockey VR - air hockey
12. Neon Hoops VR - basketball
13. Neon Boxing VR - boxing/fighting
14. Neon Blade VR - sword dueling/melee
15. Neon Drift VR - anti-gravity racing
16. Neon Smash VR - target striking/whack-a-mole

So I've got a solid spread across sports games like pong, golf, bowling, darts, hockey, and basketball, plus arcade classics like space shooters, rhythm games, archery, and pinball. The melee and racing games round out the variety.

Looking at what's still missing, there's a lot of untapped potential: puzzle games, fishing, platformers, card and board games, maze exploration, flight sims, and various other sports like tennis, dodgeball, soccer, and baseball. I should pick something that feels distinct from what's already there.

Brainstorming some possibilities—a Tetris-style puzzle game with 3D neon blocks you grab and arrange, a catching game where objects fly at you, laser redirection puzzles, disc golf, tennis, or skee-ball. I'm leaning toward a puzzle game since that's a solid genre that hasn't been explored yet and could really leverage VR's spatial mechanics.

**Neon Stack VR** feels like the strongest choice—a 3D block-stacking puzzle where you physically grab and rotate pieces as they fall into a grid. It's genuinely different from what's already in the portfolio, uses VR hand tracking naturally, and has real gameplay depth.

Though I'm second-guessing myself on whether there's something even better. Let me think through the core requirements: it needs to be completely new, feel native to VR, fit the holodeck aesthetic, and have mechanics that work with controllers. **Neon Siege VR** with siege weapons is tempting—you'd physically aim and fire catapults or cannons at waves of enemies, which is more active than tower defense. But that might feel too similar to Neon Bastion conceptually. **Neon Shuffle VR** as a physics-based curling game is another angle—sliding pucks down neon lanes with real momentum and scoring.

Actually, **Neon Disc VR** feels like the strongest choice. It's disc golf in VR with full flight physics—you throw holographic discs through obstacle courses with realistic spin, wind, and glide mechanics. It's genuinely different from darts because it's open-field courses with complex flight dynamics rather than point-and-aim precision. The disc golf mechanics—hyzer, anhyzer, fade—give it depth that other throwing games don't have, and it's a completely untapped genre for VR.

Let me explore a few more alternatives though. **Neon Maze VR** could work as a first-person exploration game with procedurally generated mazes and puzzle-solving, or **Neon Fishing VR** where you cast a line into a holographic ocean and reel in neon fish using controller swings. But I keep coming back to disc golf—the physics-based throwing with environmental factors feels like the most compelling VR experience.

Actually, **Neon Juggle VR** is catching my attention more. It's a physics-based juggling game where you keep neon objects airborne, starting with balls and progressing to rings and clubs, with combo scoring and trick shots. That feels genuinely unique and takes full advantage of VR hand tracking.

But I'm also drawn to **Neon Puck VR** — a shuffleboard-style game where you slide weighted pucks down neon lanes into target zones, with realistic physics for friction and curl. It's clearly different from air hockey and feels like a natural fit for the arcade setting.

Looking at what's actually missing in VR games, I'm thinking about puzzle mechanics, fishing, exploration, card and board games, platforming, carnival games, rope physics, destruction, marble runs, and mazes. **Neon Marble VR** stands out to me — a marble maze game where you tilt a platform to guide neon marbles through increasingly complex 3D layouts.

The core mechanic is holding the board with your VR controllers and tilting it to navigate obstacles, avoid holes, hit targets, and trigger gates. It's tactile and genuinely VR-native in a way that sets it apart from the throwing, striking, aiming, steering, fighting, and placement games I've already explored. This tilting interaction is completely unique for my portfolio. I'm going with this concept. I need to verify that the InputComponent API will handle the controller rotation properly for tilting the platform, and map keyboard controls in browser mode. Let me settle on **Neon Marble VR** as the final name and start building rather than continuing to iterate on the concept.

Looking at the build log, I see Neon Smash VR and Neon Drift VR were completed on 2026-05-28, with Neon Boxing VR and Neon Blade VR from the previous day. I need to figure out what the next AM build should be for 2026-05-29 by checking the build status files to see what's already been completed. I've got 16 games built so far, so I need something different for the next one. A marble maze puzzle game sounds perfect—Neon Marble VR where you physically tilt a holographic board to guide a glowing marble through neon labyrinths with physics-based rolling, traps, teleporters, switches, and collectibles. This'll be my first puzzle game for the portfolio. Now I need to read section 4b of the IWSDK skill file for PanelUI details, initialize the status file, and start building.

Good, I have a thorough understanding of the PanelUI system now. Let me plan the new game.

Looking at the portfolio, the genres covered are:
- Sports: pong, golf, bowling, darts, hockey, basketball, boxing
- Arcade: space shooter, rhythm, archery, pinball, target smashing
- Strategy: tower defense, billiards/pool
- Combat: sword dueling
- Racing: anti-gravity racing

I'll go with **Neon Marble VR** - a marble maze puzzle game. This is a completely new genre (puzzle/spatial manipulation) for the portfolio.

Concept:


A holographic game board floating in the holodeck that players tilt using VR controllers or keyboard to roll a glowing neon marble through maze corridors, avoiding holes and collecting gems while navigating toward the goal. The game features teleporters, moving walls, switches, and special zones like ice and boost areas to add complexity. I'm planning multiple game modes including a campaign with 12+ levels of increasing difficulty, a time attack speedrun mode, a zen mode without time pressure, and an endless mode.

I'm also considering procedurally generated mazes and a daily challenge with seeded layouts. The tilt mechanic feels natural for VR, and keyboard controls map WASD or arrow keys to board angles for browser play.

Now I'm thinking about the most efficient way to build this. There's been an issue with the scaffold tool failing since late May, so I'll copy from a recent successful game build like neon-smash or neon-drift and adapt it as my template rather than starting from scratch.

Looking at what's worked before, I should set up Node 20 first, then copy the template structure and replace the game logic with the marble maze implementation.

item.arguments:
{"content":"# Neon Marble VR

A holodeck-themed VR marble maze puzzle game built with [IWSDK](https://iwsdk.dev) 0.4.1.

Tilt the holographic game board to roll a glowing marble through neon labyrinths. Collect gems, avoid pit traps, use teleporters, slide across ice, and hit boost pads to reach the goal.

## Play

**Live:** [https://ellyz2426.github.io/neon-marble/](https://ellyz2426.github.io/neon-marble/)

## Features

- **12 handcrafted maze levels** with progressive difficulty
- **4 game modes:** Campaign (3 lives), Time Attack (race the par), Zen (no pressure), Daily Challenge (seeded daily)
- **Physics-based marble** rolling with tilt controls and wall collision
- **6 tile types:** Walls, holes (traps), gems (collectibles), teleporters, ice zones (low friction), boost pads
- **5 board themes:** Neon Holodeck, Crimson Grid, Toxic Neon, Ultra Violet, Solar Blaze
- **20 achievements** with localStorage persistence
- **Leaderboard** (top 20 scores)
- **13 PanelUI spatial UI templates**, zero HTML DOM overlays
- **Dual-runtime:** Full VR (Meta Quest) + browser-first with keyboard controls
- **Procedural Web Audio:** 12+ SFX (bounce, roll, gem collect, fall, teleport, boost, countdown, goal fanfare, achievement) + ambient drone with LFO
- **Holodeck environment:** Neon grid floor/ceiling, 14 wireframe decorations, 40 ambient particles, fog, accent lights

## Controls

### Browser
| Key | Action |
|-----|--------|
| WASD / Arrow Keys | Tilt the board |
| ESC | Pause |

### VR (Meta Quest)
| Input | Action |
|-------|--------|
| Left Thumbstick | Tilt the board |
| B Button | Pause |
| Laser Pointer | Click UI buttons |

## Tile Types

| Tile | Color | Effect |
|------|-------|--------|
| Wall | Theme-colored | Blocks marble, bounces |
| Hole | Red ring | Fall in = lose a life |
| Gem | Yellow octahedron | +200 points |
| Goal | Green ring | Complete the level |
| Teleporter A | Magenta ring | Warps to Teleporter B |
| Teleporter B | Purple ring | Warps to Teleporter A |
| Ice | Blue translucent | Reduced friction |
| Boost Pad | Green with arrow | Speed boost forward |

## Game Modes

- **Campaign:** 12 levels, 3 lives, score tracking, best times
- **Time Attack:** Same levels, race against par time
- **Zen Mode:** No timer, no lives, just explore and relax
- **Daily Challenge:** Date-seeded random level, new puzzle every day

## Tech Stack

- IWSDK 0.4.1 (Immersive Web SDK)
- Three.js via super-three
- PanelUI spatial UI (@pmndrs/uikit + @iwsdk/vite-plugin-uikitml)
- Vite 7
- Web Audio API (procedural synthesis)
- TypeScript

## Project Structure

```
src/
  index.ts      — Main entry, game loop, state machine, UI binding
  types.ts      — Types, constants, 12 level definitions, achievements, state manager
  board.ts      — Board geometry, maze rendering, collision detection
  audio.ts      — Procedural Web Audio manager (12+ SFX + ambient music)
  effects.ts    — Particle system, marble trail, ambient particles, holodeck decorations
ui/
  title.uikitml       — Title screen
  modeselect.uikitml  — Mode selection (Campaign/Time Attack/Zen/Daily)
  levelselect.uikitml — Level selection grid (12 levels)
  hud.uikitml         — In-game HUD (time, gems, lives, score) — Follower head-locked
  pause.uikitml       — Pause menu
  levelcomplete.uikitml — Level completion with stats
  gameover.uikitml    — Game over screen
  leaderboard.uikitml — Top 10 scores
  achievements.uikitml — 20 achievement slots
  settings.uikitml    — Volume controls, theme selector
  help.uikitml        — Controls and gameplay guide
  toast.uikitml       — Toast notifications — Follower head-locked
  countdown.uikitml   — 3-2-1-GO countdown — Follower head-locked
```

## Stats

- **5 source files**, 2,114 lines
- **13 PanelUI templates**, 936 lines
- **3,050 total lines**
- **20 achievements**
- **12 levels**
- **5 themes**
- **Zero HTML DOM UI**

## Build

```bash
npm install
npm run build
```

## License

MIT
","path":"workspace/neon-marble/README.md"}

## 2026-05-29 — Neon Marble VR

### Round 1 (Midnight Build)
**Date:** 2026-05-29 | **Time:** 07:00–07:45 UTC (45 min)
**Files:** 5 src + 13 uikitml = 18 total | **Lines:** 3,050
**Commits:** 2 | **Deploys:** 1

**Concept:** Holodeck VR marble maze puzzle — tilt a holographic board to roll a glowing marble through neon labyrinths. First puzzle/spatial-manipulation genre for the portfolio. Completely unique interaction paradigm (board tilting) distinct from all prior games.

**Key Work:**
- Full IWSDK 0.4.1 dual-runtime (VR + browser) with `xr: { offer: 'once' }` + `canvasPointerEvents`
- 12 handcrafted maze levels with progressive difficulty (8x8 to 13x13 grids)
- Physics-based marble: gravity response to tilt angle, rolling friction, wall collision (circle-box AABB), velocity reflection with damping
- 6 tile types: walls (solid blocks with wireframe edges), holes (red-ringed pit traps), gems (rotating yellow octahedrons), teleporters (spinning magenta/purple rings, paired A/B), ice zones (reduced friction, blue translucent), boost pads (green with arrow chevrons)
- Board geometry generator from 2D grid arrays with neon grid lines and edge glow
- Board tilt controls: WASD/arrows (browser), left thumbstick (VR), smooth interpolation
- 4 game modes: Campaign (12 levels, 3 lives, score tracking, best times), Time Attack (race against par time), Zen (no timer, no lives, relaxed), Daily Challenge (date-seeded PRNG for reproducible daily puzzles)
- 5 board themes: Neon Holodeck (cyan), Crimson Grid (red), Toxic Neon (green), Ultra Violet (purple), Solar Blaze (orange) — each defines wall, floor, accent, gem, goal, marble, and glow colors
- Scoring: base 1000 per level + time bonus (50 pts/sec under par) + gem bonus (200 each) + perfect run bonus (500 all gems)
- 20 achievements with localStorage persistence
- Top 20 leaderboard (score, level, mode, time, gems, date)
- **ALL UI via PanelUI** — 13 `.uikitml` templates, zero HTML DOM:
  - Title screen, mode select (4 modes), level select grid (4x3, 12 levels with status/best times), HUD (Follower head-locked: time, gems, lives, score, level name), pause menu, level complete (stats + next/retry/menu), game over, leaderboard (10 rows), achievements (20 slots), settings (master/SFX/music volume + theme selector), help (controls + gameplay + tile types + modes), toast (Follower), countdown (Follower, 3-2-1-GO)
- XR controller input: left thumbstick tilt, B pause, laser pointer for UI
- Browser input: WASD/arrows tilt, ESC pause
- Procedural Web Audio: 12+ SFX (wall bounce, rolling sound, gem collect arpeggio, fall descending tone, goal fanfare chord, teleport swept sawtooth, boost ascending square, ice slide highpass, button click, game start ascending, game over descending, achievement 5-note, countdown tick) + ambient drone (55Hz sine + 82.5Hz triangle pad + 0.15Hz LFO)
- Particle effects: wall bounce sparks, gem collect burst, fall explosion, teleport magenta burst, level complete celebration
- Marble trail: line-based trail with 30-point buffer, additive blending
- Animated board elements: gem rotation/bob, goal ring pulse/spin, teleporter rotation, ice/boost opacity pulse
- Holodeck environment: neon grid floor/ceiling, 14 floating wireframe decorations (torus/box/sphere/cone) with rotation and bob, 40 ambient particles with drift and pulse, fog, 2 accent lights (cyan + magenta)
- 3-2-1-GO countdown sequence with audio ticks before each level
- Toast notification system for achievements, gem collection, falls, teleporter use
- Campaign progress and best times persistence (localStorage)
- GitHub repo created, deployed to GitHub Pages

- Repo: https://github.com/ellyz2426/neon-marble
